artifacts.content:
Jim,

I wanted to circle back while last week is still fresh. Unit #312 is becoming a liability, and not just on paper. You’ll remember it’s the same squad I was in during the June 14, 2023 pursuit on Hwy 210 here in Brainerd, when the intermittent electrical failure knocked the in‑car camera offline right before the collision. That gap in the video is now front and center in the civil claim the driver’s family filed against me and the City in Crow Wing County District Court. 

On top of that, the internal affairs review Mark Johnson finished on August 3, 2023, lays out the equipment issues pretty clearly. It’s hard to keep telling officers to trust their gear when I’ve had the camera and MDC die on me in that car during high‑risk calls.

What I haven’t said out loud in a meeting is that I created part of this mess by leaning on a handshake understanding with the vendor last year instead of getting a written agreement in front of Council first. We’ve basically been operating under that informal arrangement for months, and now I’m trying to clean it up while also asking them to bless a replacement for 312.

Day‑to‑day, life at home is its own circus—Jacob’s hockey, Emily studying for her licensure, Mom’s memory sliding. I’m trying to keep the work headaches from bleeding into the house, but when I’m up at 5:30 with coffee reading about that lawsuit, it’s hard not to carry it.

When you have time, I’d like your take on how much of the pursuit/camera piece we should proactively acknowledge in any future discussions, versus just focusing on mileage, downtime, and the CPV contract.

Dan

metadata.subject: Unit 312, Hwy 210 pursuit, and cleanup on the agreement

artifacts.content:
Laura,

Just getting a breather now. Tonight was rough.

Unit 312 died AGAIN, this time with Ethan in the back. I picked him up from the counselor’s office at Brainerd High after they called saying he was in pretty bad shape. We were headed from BHS over to Karen’s place on Mill Avenue, and the transmission gave out on Riverside Drive. We sat there for 47 minutes waiting on a backup squad and a tow. Watching his anxiety ramp up minute by minute in the rearview was awful, especially knowing he’s already working with Essentia’s adolescent behavioral clinic on all this.

It’s not the first time 312 has stranded me on nights this winter, but this one hit different because it was family. I kept thinking, “If this was any other kid, I’d be furious at the department for putting them in that car.”

On top of that, I’m still stewing about how little bandwidth we’ve had to look at alternatives. Honestly, we haven’t really developed many options besides “replace the car and keep muddling through everything else,” because command staff is buried in hiring, the budget, and the rest. If Council balks, the reality is we just keep doing what we’ve been doing—even though we all know it isn’t working great.

I’m home late tomorrow after Jacob’s practice at the rink, but maybe we can still get a quick walk on the Paul Bunyan trail. I need to clear my head. Emily asked again about moving to the Cities; I didn’t have much of an answer for her.

Heading home now.

Dan

Karen,

I wanted you to hear this directly from me instead of through Ethan.

On Friday night, January 19, I was covering an overnight patrol shift and got a call from the counselor at Brainerd High that Ethan was in crisis and wanted to leave. I picked him up there and started driving him back to your place on Mill. About halfway there, on Riverside Drive, my squad (Unit 312) lost the transmission. We ended up stuck on the side of the road for close to 47 minutes waiting for another car and a tow. He was already keyed up, and sitting there in the dark with flashers going did not help his nerves, given everything he’s working through with Essentia’s adolescent behavioral clinic.

I’m really sorry he had to go through that on top of everything else. I stayed with him the whole time and kept dispatch updated, but it’s not the kind of environment I want any kid in, let alone my nephew.

You know this isn’t the first time 312 has let us down. Back on October 7, 2023, when we had that domestic call on Oak Street involving you and Mark, the car’s electrical system cut out twice. I couldn’t run a warrant or OFP check from the MDC because the power kept dropping, and we had to rely on radio. That delay came up again in the family court hearing on October 23 in Crow Wing County.

I’m pushing hard to get that unit retired. In the meantime, if Ethan ever needs a ride again, I’ll make sure it’s in a different car.

Dan

Personal log – fleet, family, and everything in between

I keep coming back to how much baggage is tied up in Unit 312.

June 14, 2023 – Hwy 210 pursuit, camera dies just before the collision. Now there’s a civil claim in Crow Wing County with my name on it, and the IA Mark Johnson finished on 8/3/23 spells out the electrical mess.

Oct 7, 2023 – domestic on Oak Street with Mark and Karen. Radio and MDC power cut out twice in 312, delaying the warrant/OFP check that later got dragged into family court on 10/23.

Jan–Feb 2024 – three breakdowns on overnight shifts. Worst was 1/19/24, when the transmission quit on Riverside while I was taking Ethan from the Brainerd High counselor’s office back to Mill Ave. Forty‑plus minutes waiting with him in the back, knowing he’s already anxious and in treatment at Essentia’s adolescent behavioral clinic.

March 2024 – 9 days straight in the city garage the same week Jensen was on admin leave over Emily’s April 2023 CLC traffic stop complaint. I ended up filling patrol gaps and canceling the March 21 Lakes Area Restorative Justice mediation with Em. I can’t put that in any official write‑up, but it’s one more way this car has bled into my personal life.

If Council shoots down the replacement again, the fallback is basically status quo: keep limping 312 along and reshuffling cars. I hate admitting it, but if they say no, we’ll probably just keep doing what we’ve been doing, even though everyone knows it’s a lousy fix.

Remember: when I talk about this publicly, stick to mileage, downtime, safety, CPV contract. Leave family, litigation, and officer names out of it.

Eric,

Catching up on a few loose ends after our August 19 sit‑down with you and Sarah at City Hall.

Thanks again for taking the time to look through the hybrid squad numbers. Since we put Units 305 and 309 into service in May 2023, I’ve been pulling the Holiday Stationstore fuel card reports for South 6th Street and matching them with odometer logs. Over that 9‑month window, those two hybrids were saving between $78 and $112 per month per car in fuel alone compared to the older Explorers. Stretch that over a 5–7 year service life and we’re realistically talking several thousand dollars in savings, not even counting the idle‑time benefits.

That’s a big part of why I’m pushing for the 2025 Ford Explorer Police Interceptor Utility hybrid as the replacement for 312. Between the maintenance headaches, the safety issues we’ve both heard about, and the fuel data, it’s the logical choice.

I’ll be honest: I’m asking Council to move on this even though I haven’t had the luxury of walking every operational detail through with my whole command staff. We’re all juggling hiring, policy updates, and the usual fire drills. I’d rather get the authorization in place and fine‑tune the deployment plan as we go than lose another year to supply chain delays.

On the home front, Emily’s still debating whether to stay here with Crow Wing County or head to the Cities, Jacob’s buried in applications to St. Cloud and Bemidji, and Mom’s memory lapses seem to be coming a little more often. That Sunday grocery run to Baxter is now non‑negotiable.

Let me know if you want any of the raw spreadsheets for your files.

Dan

metadata.subject: Follow‑up on hybrid squad fuel data and 312 replacement

artifacts.content:
Scott,

Appreciate you making time on August 19 at the Crow Wing County Law Enforcement Center. It was helpful to hear straight from you what your folks are dealing with.

I did kick around the idea of leaning more on CCSO for mutual aid instead of pushing a 2025 replacement for Unit 312 this year, but after our talk it’s pretty clear that’s not realistic. When you said you’re already holding over deputies on OT two or three nights a week just to cover your own rural township calls, that sealed it for me. Using your people as a routine backfill for our fleet shortages in the City would just drive your overtime higher and slow response outside city limits.

Same conclusion after my 12‑hour overnight ride‑along on July 3–4 with Megan Lewis. We had that domestic on Oak Street where backup from Brian Jensen in 312 was delayed about six minutes because his squad stalled near Gregory Park. That’s with three cars up. Cutting down to two after midnight to “save” enough to offset a squad purchase might look good on a spreadsheet, but on the street it’s not safe—especially around the Fourth when downtown and the Brainerd Lakes hotels are hopping.

I’m also trying to be realistic about community reaction. Whatever option we land on, some residents who already think we overreach are going to be irritated. At some point they’re just going to have to adjust to the idea that safe staffing and reliable squads cost money.

Hope you’re getting at least a few nights at the cabin before summer’s gone.

Dan

metadata.subject: Mutual aid, Unit 312, and July 3–4 coverage
metadata.location: Crow Wing County Law Enforcement Center

artifacts.content:
Event: Knee surgery – Essentia Health–St. Joseph’s Medical Center
Date: March 3, 2026
Time: 6:30 a.m. check‑in (OR sometime mid‑morning)
Location: Essentia–St. Joe’s, Brainerd – surgical wing
Attendees: Daniel Carlson; Laura (driving)

Notes:

– Procedure is left knee surgery with Dr. Michael Peterson, tied to the work‑related injury from the Laurel Street arrest on November 21, 2024. Expect 6–8 weeks on restricted duty afterward.

– Goal is to have the replacement order for Unit 312 (2025 Ford Explorer PI Utility hybrid) signed before this date so there’s no lag getting the new car into service for the east‑side / South 6th Street patrol area while I’m out.

– Still thinking about the October 12, 2025 armed domestic at 418 Maple Street. Transmission in 312 slipped twice running Code 3; in‑car camera shows a 6–7 second delay in acceleration turning onto Washington. Mark Jensen and I wrote that up in a memo on 10/14/25 after Mills Ford warned a full transmission replacement could run $6,000–$7,500 with no guarantees because of the old 2021 pursuit crash damage on Hwy 371. That call convinced me we can’t keep gambling with that car.

– Practical stuff: arrange for someone else to cover the Sunday grocery/yardwork run to Mom’s place in Baxter for at least the first month post‑op. Talk with Mark about checking in on her if she has another fall.

– This whole thing is more “nice to have fixed” than mission‑critical in the very short term, but I’d rather not be worrying about fleet decisions while I’m on pain meds.

metadata.title: Knee surgery + Unit 312 timing
metadata.location: Essentia Health–St. Joseph’s Medical Center, Brainerd

artifacts.content:
Laura,

Quick update while I’ve got a minute between meetings.

Dr. Nelson at Essentia–Brainerd Clinic finally pinned down the dates for the cardiac stress test follow‑up and possible stent. The procedure itself is set for March 19, 2025, so I put in for medical leave March 18–29. I’ve already told Mark Jensen he’ll be Acting Chief and will need to sign off on the new squad purchase order while I’m out.

I’m trying to get the 2025 Ford Explorer hybrid replacement for Unit 312 across the finish line before we hit that window. Between the June 3, 2024 breakdown on Hwy 371 when I was trying to get to St. Joe’s after Mom’s fall at the Mississippi Parkway assisted living (sat in the left turn lane, $327 tow from Northland, showed up almost an hour late and missed the neurologist’s first talk about her dementia) and the January 7, 2025 welfare check at Good Samaritan–Bethany where our guys ended up transporting her in the back of 312 after she wandered, I’m pretty much done seeing that car anywhere near family.

On top of that, Mom’s VA neurologist in St. Cloud told us back on January 9, 2026, to start planning on assisted living in the next 12–18 months. I know that’s technically “future Dan’s problem,” but it’s hanging over everything we plan.

I’m pushing to get this squad request in front of Council now, even though I haven’t had time to pull together every spreadsheet and alternative. Part of it is just making sure it’s on the record that we asked, even if they decide to punt it a year.

Let’s talk tonight after our walk on the trail if the weather holds.

Love,
Dan

metadata.subject: Surgery dates, Mom, and that damn squad car

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Carlson <dcarlson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Laura Carlson <laura.carlson@email.com>
Date: November 19, 2023 8:12 PM
Subject: Patrol shifts, Mom, and this whole 312 situation

Laura,

I wanted to write this down because I know I’ve been distracted and short on sleep.

When the overtime budget got tight in November, I agreed to cover two overnight patrol shifts in Unit #312 on November 10 and 18 so we wouldn’t blow past the OT cap. On paper it made sense, but in reality it meant being out all night in that aging 2015 Ford Explorer right after Mom’s scare.

Ruth’s fall at the assisted living facility on November 7 and the trip to Essentia Health–St. Joseph’s Medical Center in Brainerd shook me more than I admitted. She was just discharged that day, and instead of being fully available in case something went sideways at her place, I was rolling around town in #312 trying to save the city a few dollars.

What worries me is that if Unit #312 finally suffers a catastrophic failure and we’re juggling cars at the last minute, I’ll be the one filling the gaps again. That might be the job, but it directly complicates my ability to be present for family medical emergencies. Between Mom’s situation and everything with Jacob this year, I feel like I’m constantly choosing between the department and home.

We can talk more tomorrow, but I wanted you to know I see the strain this is putting on us, and I’m trying to be more intentional about how I say yes to these shifts.

Dan

metadata.subject: Patrol shifts, Mom, and this whole 312 situation

artifacts.content:
From: Chief Daniel Carlson <dcarlson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Dr. Andrea Miller <andrea.miller@brainerdpd.org>
Date: March 12, 2024 9:06 AM
Subject: Follow‑up on Tyler’s concerns about Unit #312

Andrea,

Thanks again for making time yesterday for the critical incident debrief with Officer Tyler Nguyen. I wanted to capture a few details while they’re fresh and get your perspective on how we factor this into our fleet discussions.

As we discussed on March 11, 2024 here at the Brainerd Police Department, Tyler described the January 29, 2024 welfare check on a suicidal subject near the Mississippi River bridge in Brainerd. During that call, the in‑car computer in Unit #312 froze for about 4 minutes. Taken in isolation, a 4‑minute MDC freeze is a technical glitch; in context, with his prior critical incident in 2022 and the nature of the call, it clearly landed differently.

He said he already felt keyed‑up rolling to a suicidal subject and that when the screen locked he “couldn’t trust the car” when he needed it most. My concern is that continuing to rely on a vehicle officers associate with that kind of failure can quietly erode their confidence and mental well‑being, especially for newer folks like Tyler who don’t have decades of experience to fall back on.

I’m drafting a memo on alternatives/options for replacing the 2015 Ford Explorer (Unit #312), but I’m debating how explicitly to reference the psychological impact versus keeping the formal document more technical. Any guidance on language that acknowledges these effects on officers without over‑disclosing Tyler’s situation would be helpful.

Thanks,
Dan

metadata.subject: Follow‑up on Tyler’s concerns about Unit #312

artifacts.content:
From: Chief Daniel Carlson <dcarlson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: City Administrator; Mayor and Council
Date: January 21, 2025 3:37 PM
Subject: Squad 312 replacement – timing, auction, and upcoming travel

Mayor and Council,

Attached is the formal staff report, but I wanted to provide some narrative context on the recommended motion regarding the replacement of our 2015 Ford Explorer (Unit #312).

I’m recommending you adopt a resolution authorizing the purchase of one 2025 Ford Explorer Police Interceptor Utility squad vehicle and associated equipment in the amount of $58,940.27 through the Minnesota State CPV contract. Timely approval is important. I’ve already committed approximately $1,200 out‑of‑pocket in non‑reimbursable travel and lodging for the FBI National Academy Session 261 alumni use‑of‑force symposium in Quantico on May 5–7, 2025, and I need this capital item finalized before I’m out of state.

The motion also declares Unit #312 surplus and authorizes sale at public auction. Operationally, we’re trying to coordinate that auction so it does not overlap with my mandatory Minnesota Chiefs of Police Association ethics remedial training in St. Paul on April 10–11, 2025, which I’m required to attend following the internal affairs finding last fall regarding my off‑duty conduct at the Arrowwood Lodge conference. I want to be on‑site when the vehicle is removed from service, given its history and the role it has played in several recent incidents.

I recognize the sensitivity around these issues and have kept the formal motion focused on operational needs and fiscal stewardship. I’m available to answer questions individually before the meeting.

Respectfully,

Chief Daniel R. Carlson
City of Brainerd Police Department

metadata.subject: Squad 312 replacement – timing, auction, and upcoming travel

artifacts.content:
From: Chief Daniel Carlson <dcarlson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: City Administrator; Mayor & Council
Date: February 26, 2024
Subject: Fleet Replacement – Resolution Language for March 4 Agenda

All,

Attached is the staff report for the March 4 council meeting regarding the proposed squad replacement. I’d appreciate any feedback on the draft Recommended Action Motion before it goes in the packet.

As written, the Recommended Action Motion reads:

“Motion to adopt the resolution authorizing the purchase of the 2025 Ford Explorer Police Interceptor Utility through the State CPV contract, with the understanding that the current 2015 Ford Explorer (Unit #312) be declared surplus and sold at public auction to help offset unexpected personal legal expenses I incurred after the February 3, 2024 officer‑involved crash involving my son Jacob Carlson’s DUI arrest on Highway 210, which has strained our family finances.”

I want to be very clear: I drafted that exact language myself as a thought exercise while I was working through the numbers, and I realize it is entirely inappropriate for a formal motion. My personal situation should not, and will not, factor into the City of Brainerd Police Department’s asset disposition decisions. Unit #312 should be treated like any other surplus squad, with proceeds going into the city’s general fund or the standard fleet replacement line.

I’ve removed that language from the public version of the staff report, but I felt it was important to be transparent with you about the pressure I’m feeling from the personal legal expenses tied to Jacob’s DUI and the Highway 210 officer‑involved crash. I remain committed to keeping that separate from departmental recommendations.

Respectfully,

Chief Daniel Carlson
City of Brainerd Police Department

metadata.subject: Fleet Replacement – Resolution Language for March 4 Agenda

artifacts.content:
Conversation: Messages between Daniel Carlson and Mark Carlson
Date: February 28, 2024

Daniel: Hey, you got a minute to talk later tonight?

Mark: Yeah, what’s up? Everything okay with Mom?

Daniel: Mom is about the same. This is more about Jacob and the fallout from the Feb 3 mess on 210.

Mark: The Highway 210 DUI thing? I figured the officer‑involved crash part would make it ten times worse. How bad are the lawyer bills?

Daniel: Bad. Between his DUI arrest, the crash investigation, and the separate counsel I needed because of my role as Chief of Police, the personal legal expenses are piling up. It’s putting real strain on our family finances. Laura and I have been staring at spreadsheets every night.

Mark: I’m sorry, Dan. I know you’re trying to keep the City of Brainerd Police Department stuff separate, but it all bleeds together.

Daniel: That’s exactly the problem. I actually caught myself drafting a Recommended Action Motion at work that would’ve tied the sale of the old 2015 Ford Explorer (Unit #312) to helping offset my own legal costs. I wrote: “declare Unit 312 surplus and sell it at public auction to help offset unexpected personal legal expenses I incurred after the February 3, 2024 officer‑involved crash involving Jacob Carlson’s DUI arrest on Highway 210.” Then I deleted it. Totally inappropriate.

Mark: Good call. You can’t mix those worlds, even if the money would help.

Daniel: I know. I’m sticking with a straight CPV contract purchase of the 2025 Ford Explorer Police Interceptor Utility and standard surplus procedure. My mess is mine to figure out.

Mark: We’ll figure it out. Call me after 8.

Daniel: Will do. Thanks.
